title: Ink on Cotton
stainChemistry: Dye stains (ink, markers, food coloring, dye transfer) are pigment molecules suspended in binders or vehicles. Fresh dyes are reversible; aging allows cross-linking with the fiber. VDS (volatile dry solvent) dissolves the pigment and binder, lifting the stain from the fiber. Amyl acetate is stronger but riskier on delicate fabrics. H₂O₂ 6% provides an oxidizing alternative, especially for natural food dyes. Sodium hydrosulfite reduces the dye's chromophores, eliminating color without bleaching the base fiber.
whyThisWorks: Ink is a dye in an oil-based carrier designed to permanently mark surfaces. Cotton absorbs quickly and holds stains deep in the weave. Alcohol or solvent dissolves the carrier holding the dye in place, letting you lift it away before it bonds permanently.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply VDS to dissolve the dye pigment and its binder. VDS is gentle but effective — it lifts color molecules from the fiber. Blot with clean cloth, replacing often as it picks up dye. Work from outside in. Dwell 5–10 minutes.

2. If VDS plateaus, switch to amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. This targets dye molecules that have bonded more deeply to the fiber. Test on a hidden area first. Never use on acetate fabrics. Dwell 3–5 minutes.
3. Flush with NSD to remove all solvent residue and any remaining pigment particles. Rinse thoroughly with cool water. Dwell 5 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — water causes dye to absorb deeper; treat dry-side first before any wet treatment
warnings: Using water first — water causes dye to absorb deeper into the fiber
Rubbing or agitating the stain — spreads the dye throughout the fabric
Not testing on hidden areas first — some dyes transfer and re-stain adjacent fibers
Oversaturating with solvent — creates a dye-solvent slurry harder to remove than the original stain

steps: 1. Dab the stain with a cloth dipped in rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol 91%, also called IPA).
2. Work from the outside edge toward the center. Swap to a clean part of the cloth often.
3. Keep dabbing until no more color transfers to the cloth.
5. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: For permanent markers or set-in dyes, a professional cleaner has access to specialized solvents (like amyl acetate) that work better than household rubbing alcohol. Don't keep treating — you risk damaging the fabric.
warnings: Always blot — never rub with pressure. Rubbing the stain pushes it deeper into the fabric.
Use a white cloth to avoid transferring dye from a colored cloth.
Rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol / IPA) works on most dye stains because it dissolves the carrier that holds the pigment.

neverDo: Apply water first — causes dye to absorb deeper into the fiber
Rub or agitate the stain — spreads the dye throughout the fabric
Use heat before the dye is removed — polymerizes dye into the fiber
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates solvents well. Acetate fibers cannot tolerate amyl acetate or solvents — VDS only. For colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam before use.

escalation.when: VDS plateau reached and dye has likely covalently bonded to the fiber
escalation.whatToTell: Ink stain on cotton, dye-based. Treated at home with rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol, IPA). Please use amyl acetate or professional solvent protocol.

contextQuestions: Is this ballpoint, gel pen, marker, or printer ink?
Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Permanent marker and gel pen use different chemical formulations than ballpoint — may require stronger solvent. Dryer exposure makes removal very difficult. Fresh stains respond significantly better. On colored cotton, test peroxide first.
